questions.question: The Wounded Knee Massacre of December 29, 1890, was one of the last major confrontations of the Indian Wars. What happened?
options: Lakota warriors ambushed a US cavalry column escorting settlers, killing 200 soldiers in the last major Native American military victory
US 7th Cavalry soldiers killed approximately 250–300 Lakota Sioux men, women, and children at Wounded Knee Creek, South Dakota
A peaceful Ghost Dance ceremony was disrupted by federal marshals, triggering a four-day siege that ended in a negotiated treaty
Sitting Bull was killed during a skirmish, triggering a revenge attack by Lakota warriors against a cavalry outpost
questions.answer: US 7th Cavalry soldiers killed approximately 250–300 Lakota Sioux men, women, and children at Wounded Knee Creek, South Dakota
questions.explanation: Wounded Knee Creek, South Dakota, December 29, 1890: the 7th Cavalry disarmed a band of Miniconjou Lakota led by Chief Big Foot. When a deaf tribesman resisted giving up his rifle, shooting broke out. Soldiers with Hotchkiss mountain guns then fired into the encampment. About 250–300 Lakota — including many women and children — were killed. Twenty soldiers also died, many from friendly fire. The massacre effectively ended armed Native American resistance in the Great Plains.
questions.memory_hook: Wounded Knee (December 29, 1890) killed 250–300 Lakota men, women, and children — many shot by Hotchkiss guns while running — ending Great Plains resistance; 20 soldiers died, mostly from friendly fire.

questions.question: The Bretton Woods Conference of 1944 established the international monetary framework for the postwar world. What institutions did it create?
options: The United Nations, the Security Council, and the International Atomic Energy Agency
The International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank, and established the US dollar as the world's reserve currency linked to gold
The General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) and the World Trade Organization (WTO)
The European Economic Community and the Marshall Plan administration framework
questions.answer: The International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank, and established the US dollar as the world's reserve currency linked to gold
questions.explanation: Meeting at the Mount Washington Hotel in Bretton Woods, New Hampshire in July 1944, representatives of 44 Allied nations created the IMF (to stabilize exchange rates and provide emergency loans) and the World Bank (to finance reconstruction). Crucially, they fixed all currencies to the US dollar, which was in turn fixed to gold at $35/ounce — the Bretton Woods system. This system collapsed in 1971 when Nixon ended dollar-gold convertibility ('Nixon Shock').
questions.memory_hook: Bretton Woods (1944) created the IMF and World Bank and made the dollar the world's reserve currency tied to gold at $35/oz — the system held for 27 years until Nixon severed the gold link in 1971.

questions.question: The Opium Wars (1839–1842 and 1856–1860) between China and Britain had lasting consequences for China. What was their fundamental cause?
options: Britain wanted to open China's markets by force after China refused all trade and diplomatic relations
Britain defended its profitable opium trade with China against the Qing government's attempt to end the drug trade that was causing massive social harm
China invaded British-controlled India to seize opium production territories, triggering British retaliation
A dispute over Christian missionaries operating in China escalated into full-scale war
questions.answer: Britain defended its profitable opium trade with China against the Qing government's attempt to end the drug trade that was causing massive social harm
questions.explanation: The British East India Company grew opium in India and sold it in China, creating millions of addicts and draining China's silver reserves. When Qing commissioner Lin Zexu confiscated and destroyed 1,400 tonnes of British opium in 1839, Britain responded with military force. The resulting 'Unequal Treaties' opened treaty ports to Western trade, ceded Hong Kong to Britain, and granted foreigners immunity from Chinese law — inaugurating China's 'Century of Humiliation.'
questions.memory_hook: The Opium Wars began when China tried to stop Britain profiting from mass Chinese addiction — the resulting Unequal Treaties began China's 'Century of Humiliation' and gave Britain Hong Kong.

questions.question: The modern Olympic marathon distance of 42.195 km (26.2 miles) was standardized at the 1908 London Olympics. How was this unusual distance determined?
options: It was chosen to exactly match the distance from Marathon to Athens run by Pheidippides in 490 BC, as measured by modern surveyors
It was set so the race would finish directly in front of the royal box at Windsor Castle, accommodating the start at Windsor and the finish at the Olympic stadium
The distance was calculated as the average of the race distances used at the 1896, 1900, 1904, and 1906 Olympics
It was chosen because 42 km was considered the longest distance a human could run without serious medical risk, with 195 meters added as a safety buffer
questions.answer: It was set so the race would finish directly in front of the royal box at Windsor Castle, accommodating the start at Windsor and the finish at the Olympic stadium
questions.explanation: The 1908 London marathon started at Windsor Castle (so the royal children could watch from the nursery window) and was to finish in front of the royal box inside the Olympic stadium — a distance that worked out to 26 miles 385 yards (42.195 km). This specific distance was then standardized internationally in 1921. The finish of that 1908 race also produced one of the Olympics' most dramatic moments: Italian runner Dorando Pietri collapsed repeatedly near the finish and was controversially helped across the line by officials, leading to his disqualification.
questions.memory_hook: The marathon is 42.195 km because the 1908 London race was laid out to start under the Windsor Castle nursery window and finish before the royal box — officialdom then standardized this arbitrary royal convenience.

questions.question: The French Open (Roland Garros) is the only Grand Slam played on clay. Which player holds the record for the most French Open singles titles?
options: Roger Federer with 9 titles
Rafael Nadal with 14 titles
Bjorn Borg with 8 titles
Novak Djokovic with 7 titles
questions.explanation: Rafael Nadal won the French Open 14 times (2005–2008, 2010–2014, 2017–2019, 2020, 2022), losing at Roland Garros only three times across his entire career. His clay court record over his career was approximately 450–37 — a winning percentage above 92%. He is commonly called 'The King of Clay' and many consider his record at Roland Garros the greatest individual achievement on a specific surface in all of sports.
questions.memory_hook: Nadal won Roland Garros 14 times and lost there just three times in his career — his clay court record of ~92% winning percentage makes it arguably the most dominant performance on a single surface in sports history.

questions.question: Hayao Miyazaki's 1997 film Princess Mononoke was the highest-grossing Japanese film ever at its release. What themes does it explore?
options: The tension between industrial civilization and the natural world, told through the eyes of a young prince caught between a forest god's curse and a human iron-working settlement
A young girl's coming-of-age story in 1950s Japan, balancing traditional values with the temptation of Western modernity
A submarine crew's underwater journey through mythical sea creatures, exploring Japan's fishing culture and oceanic mythology
An elderly woman's journey to return the souls of the dead to the spirit world before they cause catastrophe among the living
questions.answer: The tension between industrial civilization and the natural world, told through the eyes of a young prince caught between a forest god's curse and a human iron-working settlement
questions.explanation: Prince Ashitaka, cursed by a boar-god dying of an iron bullet's infection, travels west and finds himself between Irontown — a human settlement of women and lepers that mines iron and destroys forests — and the forest gods (including the wolf-raised San, 'Princess Mononoke'). Miyazaki presents both sides sympathetically: humans need iron to survive and liberate themselves; the forest has its own right to exist. The film offers no easy resolution — only the possibility of coexistence.
questions.memory_hook: Princess Mononoke refuses to make either humans or nature the villain — both have legitimate claims — and Miyazaki's refusal to offer easy answers distinguishes it from almost every other environmentalist film.

questions.question: Akira Kurosawa's 1954 epic Seven Samurai is widely considered the greatest action film ever made and has been remade countless times. What is its central narrative?
options: Seven ronin (masterless samurai) compete for dominance over a feudal Japanese province after its lord is assassinated
A poor farming village hires seven ronin to defend them against bandits who raid their harvest each year
Seven samurai travel together on a pilgrimage and encounter supernatural forces that test their honor and skill
A dying shogun assembles seven legendary samurai for a final battle to protect the imperial line
questions.answer: A poor farming village hires seven ronin to defend them against bandits who raid their harvest each year
questions.explanation: Set in 16th-century Japan, a village that cannot defend itself recruits seven wandering samurai (five skilled fighters and two less traditional members) to defend them against bandits who will return after harvest. The film explores class tension (samurai look down on farmers; farmers fear the samurai as much as the bandits), sacrifice, and the question of what warriors fight for. It was remade as The Magnificent Seven (1960) and has influenced countless Westerns, war films, and action films.
questions.memory_hook: Seven Samurai (1954): a village too poor to fight hires seven ronin to defend their harvest — the template Kurosawa created (outsiders defending the helpless, at personal cost) has been remade in every genre and culture since.

questions.question: Andrei Tarkovsky's 1972 Soviet science fiction film Solaris is often compared to 2001: A Space Odyssey. What philosophical question does it explore?
options: Whether artificial intelligence can be programmed to experience human emotion and ultimately revolt against its creators
Whether human consciousness can survive the physical death of the body in a purely materialist universe
Whether humanity is capable of truly understanding an alien intelligence — or whether we can only project our own psychology onto the unknown
Whether time travel would allow humans to correct their worst mistakes, or only create new catastrophes
questions.answer: Whether humanity is capable of truly understanding an alien intelligence — or whether we can only project our own psychology onto the unknown
questions.explanation: On a space station orbiting the ocean planet Solaris, the ocean appears to manifest the astronauts' repressed memories and guilt as physical beings. Rather than a story of alien contact, it becomes a meditation on the limits of human understanding — we cannot perceive the 'other' except through the lens of our own psychology. Tarkovsky himself said the film was about the impossibility of human beings truly knowing anything outside themselves. It runs nearly 3 hours and is deeply contemplative.
questions.memory_hook: Tarkovsky's Solaris (1972) uses an ocean that manifests human memories to ask whether we can ever truly understand anything alien — or whether we're condemned to only ever see reflections of ourselves.

questions.question: The Volga River is Russia's most important river and is called 'Mother Volga' by Russians. What makes it so central to Russian history and economy?
options: It flows east to west, connecting the Ural Mountains to the Baltic Sea and creating Russia's main east-west trade route
It is Europe's longest river, flowing south through Russia's heartland to the Caspian Sea, draining 40% of European Russia and historically anchoring Russian civilization
It connects Moscow to the Arctic Ocean, making it the only navigable route for Siberian trade to reach Western Europe
It flows from the Caucasus Mountains north to Moscow, providing drinking water for over 100 million Russians
questions.answer: It is Europe's longest river, flowing south through Russia's heartland to the Caspian Sea, draining 40% of European Russia and historically anchoring Russian civilization
questions.explanation: The Volga (3,692 km) flows south from the Valdai Hills north of Moscow to the Caspian Sea. It drains roughly 40% of European Russia and passes through or near Russia's major population centers — including Nizhny Novgorod, Kazan, Saratov, and Volgograd (site of the decisive WWII Battle of Stalingrad). The Volga-Don Canal connects it to the Black Sea, making it part of an inland waterway system linking five seas.
questions.memory_hook: The Volga flows 3,692 km through Russia's heartland to the Caspian Sea — 40% of European Russia drains into it, and Stalingrad (Volgograd) on its banks was the turning point of WWII.

questions.question: The Strait of Malacca is one of the world's most critical shipping chokepoints. Why is it so strategically important?
options: It is the only sea route connecting the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans without going through the Panama Canal
It connects the Indian Ocean to the South China Sea and carries roughly one-quarter of all world trade — including most of China, Japan, and South Korea's energy imports
It is the world's only natural strait wide enough for fully loaded supertankers, making it irreplaceable for oil shipping
It separates Australia from Southeast Asia and controls access to Australia's northern ports
questions.answer: It connects the Indian Ocean to the South China Sea and carries roughly one-quarter of all world trade — including most of China, Japan, and South Korea's energy imports
questions.explanation: The Strait of Malacca runs about 900 km between the Malay Peninsula and the island of Sumatra, connecting the Indian Ocean to the South China Sea. Approximately 80,000 ships pass through annually, carrying about 25% of world trade — including over 80% of China's and Japan's oil imports from the Middle East. At its narrowest point (the Phillips Channel), it is only 2.8 km wide. Its strategic importance has made it a focus of piracy, naval security, and geopolitical competition.
questions.memory_hook: The Strait of Malacca is 2.8 km wide at its narrowest and carries 25% of world trade — block it and you cut off 80% of China's and Japan's oil imports from the Middle East simultaneously.

questions.question: The CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing tool, adapted from a bacterial immune system, has revolutionized molecular biology. What can it do that previous genetic tools could not?
options: It can edit genes without any cellular machinery — working even on dead tissue and preserved specimens
It allows precise, targeted cuts at specific DNA sequences, enabling efficient editing, deletion, or insertion of genes in any organism
It can replicate the entire human genome in a test tube, providing unlimited genetic material for research
It can transfer genes between animals of different species with no risk of immune rejection
questions.answer: It allows precise, targeted cuts at specific DNA sequences, enabling efficient editing, deletion, or insertion of genes in any organism
questions.explanation: CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) uses a guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, where it makes a precise double-strand cut. Previous gene-editing tools (zinc fingers, TALENs) could do this but were expensive and slow to design; CRISPR is cheap, fast, and accurate. Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for adapting CRISPR from bacterial immunity into a gene-editing tool.
questions.memory_hook: CRISPR-Cas9 uses a guide RNA like a GPS address to cut DNA at exact locations — previous tools did this but took months to design per gene; CRISPR does it in days at a fraction of the cost.

questions.question: The Standard Model of particle physics describes the fundamental particles and forces of nature. What is its key limitation?
options: It does not explain how atoms bond together to form molecules
It does not incorporate gravity — making it an incomplete description of nature that cannot be reconciled with general relativity at extreme scales
It predicts that electrons should be 1,000 times heavier than observed
It requires particles to travel faster than light, which general relativity forbids
questions.answer: It does not incorporate gravity — making it an incomplete description of nature that cannot be reconciled with general relativity at extreme scales
questions.explanation: The Standard Model describes three of the four fundamental forces (electromagnetic, strong nuclear, weak nuclear) and their carrier particles, plus the matter particles (quarks and leptons) and the Higgs boson. But it has no mechanism for gravity and breaks down under the extreme conditions near black holes or the Big Bang — where general relativity is needed. Unifying the Standard Model with general relativity is physics' greatest unsolved problem, pursued by string theory, loop quantum gravity, and other approaches.
questions.memory_hook: The Standard Model describes three forces and all matter particles brilliantly — but it has no room for gravity, so at black holes and the Big Bang it simply stops working; unifying it with Einstein remains physics' holy grail.
